answer:
Iqbal's "Visal" from *Bang-E-Dara Part 2* celebrates the long-sought union (*visal*) with the beloved as a triumphant, transformative realization—blessed by fate, it turns the nightingale's torment into radiant surrender, mirroring Sufi *kataphatic* (affirmative) theology that asserts the divine union's attainability and luminous fulfillment.[^1]

> [p-cade15] The flower whose search, O Nightingale, would make me pine,  
> By blessed fate, that very flower is finally mine.  
>   
> [p-14ce22] I was in torment, and I made the garden grieve;  
> When your rich song I heard, in shame I'd take my leave.  
>   
> [p-136e94] No restless heart was mine, but quicksilver instead,  
> Impatient for love's sweet offense, my spirit sped.  
>   
> [p-c5ed6f] In the assembly of flowers, my failure was renowned;  
> My morning was a mirror where the darkest night was found.  
>   
> [p-894b93] Within my blood-filled breast, each breath became a blade,  
> Beneath my silence, Judgment's chaos was arrayed.  
>   
> [p-553122] That turmoil in my soul has vanished from its place,  
> My song no longer burdens this green, flowered space.  
>   
> [p-ea63e5] From passion's heat, my blisters have themselves become the flame,  
> My cries of anguish now with lightning speak their name.  
>   
> [p-3a1ddc] The lustre of this love makes my dark dust a mirror bright,  
> And in it shines the image of my timeless friend's own light.  
>   
> [p-456772] By entering chains, I found my liberty,  
> My home was built upon the wreck of me.  
>   
> [p-7ea2b7] By this sun's radiant glow, my own star brightly shines,  
> Before whose path's dust, even the pale moonlight declines.  
>   
> [p-418f98] You cast a single glance, and taught surrender's art,  
> O blessed day, that set a fire to my worthless heart![^1]

In stark contrast, Ghalib's ghazal from *Diwan-e-Ghalib* laments union (*wisaal-e-yaar*) as definitively denied by fate (*qismat*), embodying *apophatic* (negative) theology: the beloved's inaccessibility defines the lover's eternal longing, rendering fulfillment illusory and sorrow inexorable.[^2]

> [p-e43078] To be with my Beloved was not my fate;  
> Had I lived longer, waiting was my state.  
>   
> [p-56a7ac] I lived upon your promise—know that was a lie;  
> I would have died from joy, had I believed it so.  
>   
> [p-3040da] From your own fragile grace, I knew the vow was weak;  
> You could not break the bond, had it been truly deep.  
>   
> [p-8f8682] Go ask my heart about your arrow, drawn halfway;  
> This constant torment would be gone, had it but pierced me through.  
>   
> [p-3735c2] What kind of friendship makes a friend a judging guide?  
> I wish one were a healer, one to grieve beside.  
>   
> [p-61ca9a] From veins of stone a blood would flow that would not cease to run;  
> This thing you call my sorrow, had it been a truer one.  
>   
> [p-c48e8d] Though sorrow takes the soul, the heart cannot escape;  
> If not the grief of love, the world's grief would take shape.  
>   
> [p-8d1800] To whom can I describe this awful night of pain?  
> What harm was there in dying, if it did not come again?  
>   
> [p-3242ab] If death brought this disgrace, why did I not just drown?  
> No funeral would be held, no tomb would have been known.  
>   
> [p-b672f7] Who could have sight of One so singular and vast?  
> If any trace of "two" existed, paths might have been crossed.  
>   
> [p-d75b62] This talk of mystic states, these words of yours, Ghalib,  
> We would have called you saint, were you not fond of wine.[^2]

Together, they form a dialectical pair: Iqbal's ecstatic *visal* affirms the mystical union's blaze ("my dark dust a mirror bright"), while Ghalib's denial ("not my fate") negates it into perpetual *firaq* (separation), evoking the via affirmativa and via negativa in Sufi contemplation of the Divine Beloved.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 27 ("Visal"), [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/27-visal/translation/).

[^2]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 204 ("Yeh na thi hamari qismat ke visaal-e-yaar hota"), [/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/204-yeh-na-thi-hamari-qismat-ke-visaal-e-yaar-hota/translation/](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/204-yeh-na-thi-hamari-qismat-ke-visaal-e-yaar-hota/translation/).
